statement.markdown: Implement the bubble sort algorithm to sort an array of integers in ascending order. The implementation should handle edge cases such as empty arrays and arrays with only one element. However, the current implementation has a logical bug that causes it to incorrectly sort the array.
statement.inputFormat: The function takes an array of integers as input.
statement.outputFormat: The function returns the sorted array of integers.
statement.notes: The implementation should have a time complexity of O(n^2) and a space complexity of O(1).

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The input array is sorted in ascending order using the bubble sort algorithm.
The input array is sorted in ascending order using the bubble sort algorithm.

hints.text: Check the termination condition of the bubble sort algorithm.

editorial.title: Correcting the Bubble Sort Algorithm
editorial.intuition: The bubble sort algorithm repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. The pass through the list is repeated until the list is sorted.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves implementing the bubble sort algorithm without any optimizations.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves implementing the bubble sort algorithm with a proper termination condition.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness involves showing that the bubble sort algorithm correctly sorts the array.
editorial.dryRun: To perform a dry run, start with an example input and step through the algorithm to identify the logical bug.
commonMistakes: Incorrect implementation of the bubble sort algorithm

functionSignature.java: public boolean bubbleSort(int[] arr, int n);
